steps.caption_md: `nums = [10, 9, 2, 5, 3, 7, 101, 18]`. At `i = 3` (value 5), only `nums[2] = 2` is smaller, so `dp[3] = dp[2] + 1 = 2` — the subsequence `[2, 5]`.
At `i = 4` (value 3), only `nums[2] = 2` is smaller (5 and 9 and 10 are not), so `dp[4] = dp[2] + 1 = 2` — the subsequence `[2, 3]`.
At `i = 5` (value 7), the earlier smaller values are `nums[2]=2` (dp=1), `nums[3]=5` (dp=2), `nums[4]=3` (dp=2). What is `dp[5]`?

predict.prompt_md: At `i = 5` (value 7), earlier smaller values give `dp[2] = 1`, `dp[3] = 2`, `dp[4] = 2`. What is `dp[5]`?

choices.label_md: `2` — just reuse the largest earlier dp value directly.
`4` — sum all three qualifying dp values plus 1.

predict.explanation_md: `dp[5] = 1 + max(dp[j] for smaller earlier values) = 1 + max(1, 2, 2) = 3`, extending either `[2, 5, 7]` or `[2, 3, 7]`.

steps.caption_md: Table complete: `dp = [1, 1, 1, 2, 2, 3, 4, 4]`. The answer is `max(dp) = 4` — e.g. `[2, 3, 7, 18]` — note it doesn't end at the last index.
